preamble: The shift from an oral vowel to its nasalized counterpart is a common phonetic process, typically occurring as regressive assimilation when followed by a nasal consonant (VN > 	ilde{V}N), often followed by the loss of the consonant itself.

examples.note: Latin 'o' followed by 'n' nasalizes to [ɔ̃] in Old French, though this example uses 'o', the process is identical for 'a'.
Latin 'a' nasalizes before 'n', followed by the loss of the nasal consonant and subsequent diphthongization.
Sanskrit 'a' followed by a nasal cluster reduces to a long nasalized vowel.

sources: Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
